Okay, I need to write a letter from a grizzled Victorian-era lighthouse keeper to his estranged daughter. The user mentioned that they haven't spoken in five years and that there's a terrible storm coming, and the lighthouse keeper isn't sure he'll survive it. Let me start by thinking about the key elements here.

First, the setting is Victorian-era, so the language should be formal and somewhat archaic. The lighthouse keeper is probably in his 60s or older, so his tone would be weathered, maybe a bit gruff, but with underlying emotion. He's writing to his daughter, who he's estranged from, so there's a mix of regret and longing.

The storm is a crucial element. It's not just a natural event; it's a metaphor for the turmoil in his life and his relationship with his daughter. The storm could also be a symbol of his impending death, which adds urgency to the letter.

I should include some details about the lighthouse itself, maybe the lamp, the fog, the isolation. These elements can help set the scene and give the letter a more vivid, atmospheric quality. Also, the lighthouse keeper's duties would be important to mention—keeping the light on, the responsibility he feels, which might tie into his feelings towards his daughter.

He hasn't spoken to his daughter in five years, so the letter should reflect that long silence. There might be reasons for their estrangement—maybe a

**[Envelope Addressed to:  
Miss Eleanor "Ellie" Whitmore  
Cottage on the Hill  
Havenshire, England  
April 5, 1898]**

---

**Havenshire Lighthouse**  
**Havenshire, England**  
**April 5, 1898**

**My Dearest Ellie,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that has long since grown accustomed to the silence of your absence. Five years have passed since the last time I saw your face, and yet the weight of your absence is heavier than the storm that now rages upon these shores.

I hope this letter finds you well, though I suspect it will find you in the same place you have always been—somewhere distant, somewhere safe, somewhere that does not require the care of a father who has failed you.

You were not born with a name, but I gave you one, and I believe it has the power to bind us, even when we are not bound by love. I have kept your old drawings in a box beneath my bed, and I have read your letters—those few that you sent me—many times. They are written in a hand that is no longer childish, but still filled with the innocence of a girl who once believed in miracles.

I have not written to you because I feared what I might say, or worse, what I might not say. I have not written because I feared

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better health than I. I write to you now not with the hope of mending what has been broken between us, but with the certainty that I may not have the chance to speak to you again. The sea has been restless these past few days, and the wind howls like a banshee in the night. I have seen the sky darken with a fury I have not known in thirty years. I fear the storm that is coming will take more than just the light from this tower.

You were a child when you left, and I was a fool to let you go. I remember the day you stood at the door, your face pale as the moon, your eyes filled with something I could not name—anger, sorrow, or perhaps both. You said you needed to see the world, and I, in my pride, allowed it. I told myself that I would not be the father who held you back. But I was wrong. I have been alone ever since.

I have kept the lighthouse, as you would have expected. The sea is a cruel mistress, but I have learned to respect her. I tend the flame, I watch the skies, and I listen to the waves. The hours are long, and the silence is heavy, but I have grown used to it. I have grown used to the ghosts of your laughter, the echo of your footsteps on the stone steps, the way the

**The Lighthouse, Blackmoor Point**  
**October 31st, 1879**  

*Dearest Eleanor,*  

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee through the cracks of this old stone tower. The sea is restless, the waves crashing against the cliffs as if in mourning. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for thirty years, but tonight the light may not be seen by any soul save the stars.  

It has been five long years since you left this house. Five years since you turned your back on your father, on the sea, on the life we shared. I have not spoken your name aloud in that time, though I have whispered it in the dark, when the wind was still and the sea was calm.  

You were a fine girl, Eleanor. Stronger than most, more stubborn than any of the sea dogs who ever came to Blackmoor Point. I remember the day you first climbed the steps to the lantern room, no older than ten. You stood there, small and proud, and said, “I shall be the best lighthouse keeper in all the world.” I laughed then, and I wept.  

But the world does not wait for promises. And neither does the sea.  

I have not seen the light of your face in years, and I fear I may never again. You were always so quick to leave, and so slow to return.

**The Lighthouse at Blackmoor Point**  
*April 13th, 1879*  

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

If you are reading this, then the sea has claimed me. Or perhaps the wind. Or the cold, which creeps into the bones like a thief in the dead of night. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for thirty years, and the light burns with a steady, unblinking glow, as if it knows the world is not yet done with me.

The sky is a bruise of black, and the sea howls like a beast wounded. The wind whips through the cracks in the stone walls, and I have not heard the sound of your voice in five years. Five. It feels like a lifetime, Eliza. A lifetime of silence, of cold, of watching the waves devour the cliffs and the tide take what it will.

I have not written to you, nor have you written to me. You left in the spring of 1874, when the storms were fierce and the light was failing. You said you could not stay, that the sea had no place for a woman, and that I was too old to understand. You called me a fool, and I let you go. I did not cry. I lit the lamp and said nothing.

But the sea has a way of returning, and so has the wind. Tonight, the storm is unlike any

**The Keeper’s Cottage**  
**Cape Lighthouse**  
**Northamptonshire**  
**April 5th, 1893**

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with a quill that trembles in my hand, and a heart that feels as though it might shatter beneath the weight of what I must say. I have not written to you in five years, and I do not know if this letter will reach you, or if it will be lost to the sea or the wind, as so many things in this life are. But I must try.

The storm is coming, and I fear it may take more than just the light from our tower. I have seen such tempests before, and I know the sea does not forgive those who do not respect her. I have been a keeper for thirty years now, and I have learned to fear her more than I fear death. But tonight, I feel something different. Something that has not been in me since the day you left.

I remember that day, Eliza. I remember the way the light blinked and the wind howled, and how I stood at the window, watching the waves crash against the rocks like a thousand mourners. You were not there. You had gone, and I had no idea where. I thought I could keep the light burning for you, even if you were not here. I thought I could keep the sea at bay, even if my heart had

**The Lighthouse, Culloden Head**  
**April 1st, 1887**  

My Dearest Eliza,  

If you are reading these words, then it is either the hand of God that has spared me, or the cruel trick of a mind grown fevered by the storm’s howling. Either way, I write with the certainty that I shall not see you again. And yet, I must. For I have spent five long years of silence, and now, with the wind shrieking like the wails of the damned, I feel the weight of it all—your absence, my guilt, the shame of a man who has failed you in every way.  

You were a child when you left, Eliza. A mere girl with eyes like the sea at dawn and a heart too kind for this cruel world. I remember the night you went, the way the lantern glowed in the distance, and how I stood there, watching you disappear into the fog, like a ghost I could not reach. I told myself I would not let the sea take you, that I would be your father, your protector, your anchor. But I was wrong. I was not enough.  

I have kept the lighthouse, as I always have, though the light has long since gone out. The lamp is broken, the oil is dry, and the sea has claimed more than its share of my days. I have seen the tempests

**April 12th, 1897**  
**Lighthouse, Isle of Skye**  

My Dearest Eleanor,

I sit here by the window, the wind howling like a banshee, the sea roiling in a fury I’ve never seen. The lantern is still burning, though I know it won’t be for long. I’ve been preparing for this storm for days, but tonight feels different. I can feel it in my bones—that something is coming, and I’m not sure I’ll be here to see the dawn.

I’ve been thinking about you. Not just in the way I always have, but with a clarity that’s been building inside me like the storm outside. Five years have passed since you left, and I’ve tried to forget what it was like to have you in my life. But I can’t. You were my world once, and now it’s just a hollow shell.

I know you don’t want to hear from me. You’ve made that clear enough. But I had to write. I had to try. I’ve spent every night since your departure wondering if I did the right thing. Did I push you away too hard? Did I fail you? I don’t know. I only know that I’ve never stopped loving you, even when you stopped speaking to me.

The lighthouse is my home, my duty, my burden. I’ve given everything to this place, and I’ve lost everything else.

**My Dearest Elara,**

I write this with a hand that trembles—not from fear, but from the weight of the words I have kept locked away for far too long. The wind howls outside like a beast in torment, and I know it will not last much longer. The sea, too, is restless, churning with a fury that mirrors the storm in my heart.

Five years, Elara. Five long, silent years. I have watched the dawn rise and set without you, and I have counted the hours between the waves, hoping one day they might carry a message I could understand. But you never came. You never wrote. You never called. And I did not dare reach out, for fear that even the smallest word might break what little was left between us.

I have always believed in the light, you know. Not just the beacon that guides the ships, but the light within—a stubborn, unyielding thing that refuses to be extinguished. I have kept it burning, even when the world seemed determined to drown it. But tonight, I fear that even the light may not be enough.

The storm is coming. I can feel it in my bones. The air is thick with the scent of salt and thunder, and the sea roars as if it knows what is to come. I have prepared the lamp, though I do not know if it will be enough. I have made my peace with the dark, though I do not know

---

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I trust this letter finds you in good health and high spirits, though I daresay I have not the heart to wish for such things at this time. The sea has been restless of late, and the wind doth howl as if in mourning. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I fear the silence has grown too long, too heavy to bear.

You were but a child when you left, and I, a man of few words and many duties, allowed the waves to take the place of our conversations. I do not know if I was wrong to let the matter rest, or if I was right to let the matter rest. I only know that the world moved on, and I, a solitary figure upon this rocky isle, did not have the heart to chase after you.

The storm that now approaches is of a most terrible nature. The skies have darkened with an ominous gloom, and the sea, which has always been my companion, now rages with a ferocity I have not seen in many a year. The light must be kept burning, as it always has been, for the safety of those who sail these waters. I have lit the lamp, and I shall tend to it with unwavering care, even as the

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it will find you in a place far removed from the one I now occupy. You have not heard from me in five years, and I am not sure if I should write at all, but the storm is coming, and I feel the weight of it pressing upon my chest like a leaden shroud. I must speak, even if it is only to the wind.

I have been the lighthouse keeper here for nearly twenty years now, and though it is a lonely life, it has given me a kind of peace I never thought I would find. The sea is a fickle mistress, and I have seen her wrath more times than I care to remember. But tonight, she is different. The sky is dark as ink, the wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars with a fury that I have not heard since the day I left you.

You were just a child then, no more than ten, and I left you with your mother, believing it was for the best. I thought I was protecting you from the truth, from the darkness that I have carried in my heart since the night I saw her fall. I thought I was doing the right thing, but I was wrong. I have spent these years in silence, in guilt, in regret, and I have never once thought of you. Not once.

I know you must be angry.

**To My Dearest Elara,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. Five years have passed since the last time you set foot in this lighthouse, and five years have been too long to bear the silence between us. The sea has been restless these past months, and now, as the moon sinks low and the wind howls like a wounded beast, I fear the night may not end as it began.

You were but a child when you left, and though I told myself I would wait for you, time has worn down the walls of this old keep and the bones of my own heart. I have kept the light burning for you, Elara—though I do not know if it is for the sea or for the hope that one day, you might return.

The storm is coming, and I have seen the signs in the sky. The clouds are black as ink, and the waves rise like the hands of some great, unseen god reaching for the shore. I have lit the lantern and set the oil to burn bright, for I know that if the light fails, the ships will not see the rocks and will be lost to the deep.

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls outside like the wails of a child, and the sea roars as though it would devour the very earth. Tonight, the storm is coming with a fury that has not been seen since the days of the great tempest of ’57. I fear I may not see the dawn, and if I do not, I hope this letter finds its way to you.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eliza. I know the time has been long, and I do not expect you to forgive me. I have not forgotten the last time we met, nor the words we exchanged that ended our bond. But I write this not as a plea for forgiveness, but as a man who has come to understand the weight of his silence and the cost it has taken from both of us.

You were once my greatest joy, my light in the darkest hours. I remember the day you were born, how the lighthouse light shone brighter than ever, as if the sea itself had come to welcome you into the world. You were a child of the light,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the weight of the sea pressing against the walls of this old keep, the wind howling like a banshee in the night. The lantern has been lit, as it always is, and the light will guide those lost at sea—though I fear the sea may claim me before dawn.

Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left. Five years since you told me I was not a father, that I had no right to call you mine. You said it with your hands, cold and unyielding, as if they could unmake the bonds of blood and duty. I did not argue. I did not beg. I simply watched you go, the way a lighthouse keeper watches the horizon, knowing the storm may come, but not knowing when.

But tonight, I am not the man I was. I have aged, as all men do, and the sea has taken more from me than I can name. The hands that once steadied the lamp now tremble. The eyes that once saw the stars now weep for the light I have lost. And yet, I remain. I remain because I have always been a man of duty, and the sea does not ask for love, only service.

I have no grand words for you, no poetry of the heart. You have never been one for sentiment, and neither am I. But I have watched the storm gather, and I fear it will take me.

**The Lighthouse Keeper’s Quarters**  
**Whitby Head, Yorkshire**  
**12th of October, 1876**  

*The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars as though it seeks vengeance upon the world. The lantern’s light flickers in the gale, and I fear it may not hold. I have not written a single letter in five years, but tonight—tonight, I must. For if I do not, I may never have the chance to speak again.*  

**To My Dearest Eliza,**  

**If you are reading this, then I am either dead, or you have found it among the wreckage of the lighthouse. Either way, I am sorry.**  

I have not spoken your name in five years, not since the day you left. I told myself it was for your own good, that the sea was too cruel, that the world was too harsh. But I was wrong. I was only afraid. Afraid of what I might say. Afraid of what I might find.  

You were ten when you last saw me. I remember the way your eyes followed me as I climbed the steps of the lighthouse, the way you clutched your mother’s hand so tightly, as if she might hold you together. I told you I would always be there, that the light would never fail. But I lied.

**The Lighthouse Keeper’s Letter**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**The Lighthouse, Stonehaven**

**Dear Eliza,**

I write this with the wind howling like a banshee and the sea roiling in a fury that makes me fear it may swallow the very rock upon which I stand. Tonight, the storm is coming, and I do not know if I shall see another dawn.

It has been five long years since you left, and I have not heard a word from you. I have sent letters, I have written again and again, but they have all gone unanswered. I have tried to forgive you, and I have tried to forget, but the pain of your absence lingers like the scent of salt in the air, ever-present, ever-aching.

I have always believed in the light, Eliza. Not just the light that guides the ships, but the light of hope, of love, of purpose. And yet, I have come to fear that the light within me has been dimmed by the darkness of your absence. I have watched the sea for years, and I have seen many storms. I have known the fury of the wind and the wrath of the waves. But no storm has ever made me feel so alone as the silence between us.

You were a child when you left this place, as I was a man who had long since forgotten how to love. The sea roars with a fury I have never known, and I fear the storm may claim more than the lighthouse tonight. Yet, I must write, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and the silence has grown heavier than the sea.

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may never reach you. The post is unreliable these days, and I have no certainty that the sea will not swallow it whole. Still, I have sent it with all the care I can muster, and I pray it reaches you before the storm breaks.

My dearest Eleanor, I have much to say, and yet I fear that the words may not be enough to mend the rift between us. I have been a poor father, a distant father, and I know it. I have spent these years in the company of the sea and the light, and not of you. I have not known how to be a man in your life, and I have not known how to be a father. I have not known how to love you as

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it may find you in one of your usual moods—bitter, unfeeling, and quite possibly nursing a glass of sherry while muttering about the "absurdity of men." I write to you now not with the hope of reconciliation, but with the grim certainty that I may not live to see the dawn. The storm is upon us, and the sea, that old beast, has grown restless.

Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left. Five years since you left me to tend this cursed lighthouse and its unrelenting light. I have not spoken your name aloud in that time, for it tastes of salt and regret and the bitter sting of a wound that refuses to close.

I know you think me a fool, a man who clings to his duties like a child to a toy. But I have seen the sea take more than one man in its wrath. I have seen it take the lives of those who thought themselves safe. And I have seen it take the lives of those who thought themselves invincible. I do not wish to die, Eleanor. But if I must, I would rather meet my end in the arms of the sea than in the cold silence of your absence.

You were a fine girl, Eleanor. Too fine for the likes of me, I suppose. You had a mind like a blade, and a heart like a

*The Lighthouse, Stormhaven Point*  
*April 3rd, 1876*  

My Dearest Eliza,  

If you are reading this, then I have not made it through the night. Or perhaps I have, and the sea has taken me to some place where the wind howls in a language only the drowned can understand.  

I write this with the last of the candle wax, the lamp’s flame flickering like a dying soul. The storm is upon us—God’s own wrath, it seems. The waves have grown like the hands of some great sea-beast, clawing at the cliffs with a fury that makes my bones tremble. I have lit the beacon, as I have done for thirty years, but I fear it may not be enough.  

You were not here when the last light went out. Not that you ever were. Five years, Eliza. Five long, bitter years since the last letter you sent me was a single line, scrawled in ink that had long since dried: *“I do not need you.”* And I did not need you, either. Not after the night you left, not after the night the sea took your voice from me.  

I have kept the lighthouse, as I always have. The rocks, the wind, the endless salt in the air—these are my only companions. The villagers speak of me in hushed tones, as if I were a ghost

*The Lighthouse, Blackmoor Point*  
*October 31st, 1873*  

*To the daughter of my old bones,*  

I write with a quill that trembles more than the sea, and with a hand that has long since forgotten the strength of youth. The wind howls like a wounded beast in the distance, and the sea, that old, black-eyed thing, roars as if it too has heard of your name.

Five years, lass. Five long, bitter years. I have kept the light burning, as I always have, through every storm, every fog, every night that the world forgot to turn. But this one—this one feels different. The sky is a bruise of grey and black, and the waves rise like the hands of the damned, clawing at the cliffs. I fear I may not see the dawn.

You were ten when you left. Ten years old, with a head of fire and a heart of ice. I remember the day as if it were yesterday. The ship came at dusk, its lanterns dim and its captain’s face like a storm cloud. You stood at the door, your hair wild as the wind, your eyes like the sea in a gale. You did not speak. You did not even look at me. And then, with a single nod, you turned and walked away, as if I were a ghost, and you had forgotten the name that

**The Lighthouse Keeper's Letter**  
**White崖 Lighthouse**  
**West Coast of England**  
**October 17th, 1898**  

My Dearest Eleanor,  

I write to you with a heart heavy as the storm clouds gathering on the horizon. The sea is restless tonight, and I fear the night may take more than just the light from this tower. I pray that this letter finds you in better health than I, and that the winds of fate have not carried you away from your own life.

Five long years have passed since the day you left, and I have not spoken your name in all that time. I have kept the lamp burning, as I always have, and I have watched the waves with a quiet sorrow, for I know that they will never take me away from this place, but they may take me before I can find a way to reach you again.

I have often wondered what you might be doing now. Are you still in London, or have you found some new path in life? I do not know if you still remember the days when we were together, when the sea was our playground and the lighthouse our home. I do not know if you still remember the scent of salt in the air, or the sound of the waves at night, or the way the light would dance on the water like a ghost of our old life.

I have thought of you often, especially when the sea was calm and the

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**April 12th, 1887**

**The Keep, Beacon Head, Devonshire**

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee through the cracks in this old stone tower, and the sea, that ever-hungry beast, roars in protest. I have not spoken your name in five long years, and yet tonight, as the storm gathers strength and the sky turns the color of a bruise, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in all that time.

I have always believed that a lighthouse keeper must be a man of quiet resolve, a man who guards the shore with steady light and unshakable heart. But I fear I have become more a relic of a bygone age than a guardian of the sea. And I fear, more than anything, that I have failed you.

You were a child when you left, no older than twelve, and I could not bear the thought of you alone in the world. I was not the father you needed, and I was not the man you deserved. I was a fool to think that distance would mend what was broken. I was a fool to believe that silence would be your salvation.

I have watched the sea for many years, and I have come to know its moods. Tonight, it is angry. It is vengeful. It is as if

**Lighthouse Keeper's Letter**

**April 12, 1897**

**To My Dearest Clara,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside the lighthouse, rattling the windows and threatening to tear the very stones from the foundation. The storm is coming—violent, unrelenting—and I fear for my life. But more than that, I fear what I must say.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of unanswered letters, of dreams that end in the same place: the sound of your footsteps fading down the pier, the echo of your voice calling back, and the door slamming shut.

I’ve tried to forget. I’ve tried to move on. But the lighthouse is a prison of my own making, and I’ve been trapped here ever since you left.

I never meant to hurt you, Clara. I never wanted to be the man who drove you away. But I was lost, and I didn’t know how to find my way back. I was a fool, and I let pride blind me. I thought I could keep the lighthouse running, keep the light burning, and somehow, I could keep the pain at bay. But the light is not just for ships—it's for those who are lost, like me.

I’ve been watching the sea all these years, hoping to see your face in the waves, hoping to hear your voice in the wind.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a hand that trembles—not from fear, but from the weight of what I have kept unsaid for so long. Five years, Eliza. Five long, silent years. I have watched the sea rage and calm, the sky shift from black to gold, and still I have said nothing. But tonight is different. Tonight, the storm comes with a fury I have not seen in my lifetime, and I do not know if I will see dawn.

I have always believed that the sea reveals the truth of a man. It does not forgive, nor does it forget. And so it is with you, my daughter. I have seen the truth of you in the waves, in the wind, in the silence between our words. You were never meant to be the daughter of a lighthouse keeper. You were meant for more. You were meant for the world beyond this rock, beyond this life I could not give you.

I know you think I abandoned you. That I left you to the mercy of the world, to the cold and the loneliness you found in your own way. But I did not leave you. I stayed here, in this endless night, because I could not bear to watch you go. You were the only thing that ever made me feel like a man worth saving. And when you left, I did not know if I was losing you or myself.

I have no grand words, no promises I can

Certainly, here's a letter written in the voice of a grizzled Victorian-era lighthouse keeper, filled with the dignity, sorrow, and resolve of a man who has spent his life in service and solitude:

---

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that has long grown accustomed to the steady rhythm of the sea, and with a heart that has grown heavy with years of silence. Five years, my child, five long, weary years since your last letter found its way to me, and I have not dared to answer. But tonight, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea threatens to reclaim the land once more, I find I must speak, even if only to the paper upon which these words shall rest.

I have spent my days in the watchtower, keeping the light burning for those who sail the dark waters. It is a duty I have taken to with reverence, and one I have never failed. Yet, I confess, there are nights when I wonder if the light I keep burning is for the sailors, or for the ghosts of those who have passed, and for the memories of a daughter who has not been seen nor heard from in far too long.

I have not spoken to you, Eleanor, not in words, not in letters, not even in the silence that has grown between us. I have been a man of duty, of discipline, of quiet endurance, and I have allowed that to become a shield, a

**[Letterhead: Lighthouse Keeper’s Quarters, Point Argent, 12th of April, 1897]**

My Dearest Elara,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that aches with the weight of years unspoken. Five years have passed since you left, and I have not dared to write, not even to myself. But tonight—tonight, the storm is coming, and I fear I may not see the morning.

You were never meant to leave. I know that now. I know that I should have held you closer, listened more, loved you better. But I was a fool, and I was afraid. Afraid of what you might have become, afraid of what I might have lost if I let you stay. I was a lighthouse keeper, not a father. I thought that distance would protect you, that silence would keep you safe. But I was wrong.

I have watched the sea rage and rage, and I have seen the night swallow the sky. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves crash against the cliffs with a fury I have never known. I have spent my life standing at the edge of the world, watching the horizon, and tonight, I feel it closing in.

I have no regrets, Elara. Not for the life I chose, not for the sacrifices I made. I have a duty, a purpose, and I would not trade it for anything. But

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. Five years have passed since the last time I laid eyes upon you, and though the days have worn on, the absence still cuts deeper than any blade.

I have kept the light burning these many nights, as I have always done, for the sea is a fickle mistress and the men who sail her are often lost to her moods. But tonight—tonight is different. The sky has turned the color of a bruise, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. I have seen such storms before, but never one like this. The sea is rising, and the waves are not mere ripples but walls of fury, crashing against the cliffs with a violence that shakes the very bones of the earth.

I do not know if I shall see dawn. And if I do not, I write this not as a man who has given up, but as a father who has not forgotten you.

Eliza, my child, I have not written because I feared what might be said. I feared that my words would be too harsh, too full of regret, too steeped in the bitterness of a man who could not hold you close when you were young. I have spent these years watching the sea, and in its endless rhythm, I have found only silence. But now, as the storm gathers, I think of you more than ever.
